statement: The epiglottis covers the trachea during swallowing to prevent aspiration.
rationale: The epiglottis is a flap of cartilage that folds over the glottic opening during swallowing. This prevents food and liquid from entering the trachea and lungs.

statement: The cricoid cartilage is the only complete ring of cartilage in the airway.
rationale: The cricoid is the only complete cartilaginous ring. It is located below the thyroid cartilage. All other tracheal cartilages are C-shaped rings open posteriorly.

statement: In children the narrowest part of the airway is at the cricoid cartilage.
rationale: In children under 8 years the narrowest point is at the cricoid below the vocal cords. In adults the narrowest point is at the glottis level of vocal cords.

statement: Snoring respirations indicate the tongue may be obstructing the airway.
rationale: Snoring occurs when the tongue partially blocks the airway in the posterior pharynx. It indicates need for airway positioning or adjunct to move the tongue forward.

statement: Gurgling respirations indicate fluid in the airway that needs suctioning.
rationale: Gurgling sounds indicate the presence of blood vomit or secretions in the airway. Suction is needed to clear the airway and prevent aspiration.

statement: Stridor is a high-pitched sound indicating upper airway obstruction.
rationale: Stridor is a harsh high-pitched sound heard primarily on inspiration. It indicates significant narrowing of the upper airway from swelling foreign body or other obstruction.

statement: Absent breath sounds on one side may indicate pneumothorax or other serious condition.
rationale: Unilateral absent or diminished breath sounds can indicate pneumothorax hemothorax or foreign body obstruction of a main bronchus. Always compare both sides.

statement: The head-tilt chin-lift is the preferred method to open the airway in trauma patients.
rationale: Head-tilt chin-lift extends the neck which could worsen spinal injury. Jaw-thrust without head extension is preferred for trauma patients with suspected spinal injury.

statement: Proper head positioning is often sufficient to open an obstructed airway.
rationale: Many airway obstructions are caused by the tongue falling back. Simply repositioning the head using head-tilt chin-lift or jaw-thrust often opens the airway without adjuncts.

statement: Infants require a neutral head position rather than hyperextension for airway opening.
rationale: Infant airways are more anterior and hyperextension can actually obstruct them. A neutral or slightly extended position sniffing position is optimal for infant airway management.

statement: An OPA that is too large can push the epiglottis down and obstruct the airway.
rationale: An oversized OPA can displace the epiglottis posteriorly causing or worsening obstruction. Proper sizing from corner of mouth to earlobe prevents this complication.

statement: The OPA is inserted with the tip toward the palate then rotated 180 degrees in adults.
rationale: In adults the OPA is inserted upside down with tip toward the hard palate then rotated 180 degrees as it passes the tongue. This prevents pushing the tongue backward.

statement: NPAs can be used in patients with suspected basilar skull fracture.
rationale: NPAs are relatively contraindicated in basilar skull fracture due to risk of intracranial placement. Signs include raccoon eyes Battle sign and CSF leakage from ears or nose.

statement: The NPA is measured from the nostril to the earlobe.
rationale: Proper NPA sizing is from the tip of the nose to the earlobe. A properly sized NPA maintains airway patency without extending too far into the pharynx.

statement: The NPA should be lubricated with a water-soluble lubricant before insertion.
rationale: Lubrication eases insertion and reduces trauma to the nasal mucosa. Use water-soluble lubricant. Insert along the floor of the nasal cavity not upward.

statement: Rigid suction catheters are preferred for suctioning the oropharynx.
rationale: Rigid Yankauer catheters are designed for oropharyngeal suctioning. They are more effective for removing thick secretions and vomit than flexible catheters.

statement: Suction should be applied continuously for as long as needed to clear the airway.
rationale: Suctioning removes oxygen and can cause hypoxia. Limit suctioning to 10-15 seconds then provide ventilation. Repeat as needed with oxygenation between attempts.

statement: Position changes can help with airway drainage if suction is not immediately available.
rationale: Turning the patient to the side can allow gravity to drain fluids from the airway. This can be a temporizing measure until suction is available.

statement: A conscious choking adult who cannot speak cough or breathe needs immediate abdominal thrusts.
rationale: Complete airway obstruction with inability to speak cough or breathe is immediately life-threatening. Perform abdominal thrusts until the object is expelled or the patient becomes unconscious.

statement: Back blows and chest thrusts are used for choking infants rather than abdominal thrusts.
rationale: Abdominal thrusts can damage infant abdominal organs. Use 5 back blows alternating with 5 chest thrusts for infant choking. Continue until object clears or infant becomes unconscious.

statement: Blind finger sweeps should be performed to remove foreign bodies from the airway.
rationale: Blind finger sweeps are not recommended as they may push objects deeper. Only remove visible objects. Look in the mouth with each airway opening during CPR for choking victim.

statement: Chest compressions during CPR may help dislodge a foreign body in an unconscious choking victim.
rationale: Chest compressions generate higher airway pressures than abdominal thrusts. When a choking patient becomes unconscious begin CPR which may dislodge the object.

statement: Inspiration is primarily an active process while normal expiration is passive.
rationale: The diaphragm and intercostals contract actively during inspiration. Normal expiration is passive from elastic recoil. Accessory muscle use for expiration indicates distress.

statement: The normal stimulus to breathe is rising carbon dioxide levels not falling oxygen.
rationale: Chemoreceptors primarily respond to rising CO2 levels. Oxygen levels must fall significantly before they drive respiration. This has implications for COPD patients.

statement: Minute volume equals respiratory rate multiplied by tidal volume.
rationale: Minute volume is the total air moved per minute calculated as rate times tidal volume. Both fast shallow breathing and slow deep breathing can produce inadequate minute volume.

statement: Respiratory rate alone is sufficient to determine if breathing is adequate.
rationale: Rate quality and depth must all be assessed. A patient breathing 20 times per minute with minimal depth may be hypoventilating. Assess the complete respiratory picture.

statement: Use of accessory muscles indicates increased work of breathing.
rationale: Accessory muscles sternocleidomastoid scalenes intercostals are recruited when normal respiratory muscles cannot meet demands. Their use indicates significant respiratory distress.

statement: Tripod positioning is assumed by patients to maximize breathing capacity.
rationale: Sitting upright and leaning forward with hands on knees tripod position maximizes use of accessory muscles and diaphragm function. Patients instinctively assume this position in distress.

statement: Paradoxical breathing where the chest and abdomen move in opposite directions may indicate fatigue.
rationale: Normal breathing shows coordinated chest and abdominal movement. When they move oppositely it can indicate respiratory muscle fatigue or impending failure.

statement: Cyanosis is a late sign of hypoxia.
rationale: Cyanosis requires significant deoxygenation to be visible approximately 5g/dL of deoxygenated hemoglobin. Other signs like tachycardia and altered mental status may appear earlier.

statement: Each ventilation should be delivered over approximately 1 second.
rationale: Slow steady breaths over 1 second allow air to reach the alveoli and reduce gastric insufflation. Fast forceful breaths are less effective and increase complications.

statement: Visible chest rise confirms adequate ventilation.
rationale: Watching for chest rise confirms air is entering the lungs. If the chest does not rise reposition the airway and retry. Excessive volume causing stomach rise should be avoided.

statement: Two-rescuer BVM ventilation is more effective than single-rescuer technique.
rationale: One rescuer can use both hands to maintain mask seal while the other squeezes the bag. This improves both seal and ventilation delivery compared to single-rescuer technique.

statement: Gastric distension from ventilation can impair breathing and increase aspiration risk.
rationale: Air in the stomach pushes up on the diaphragm limiting lung expansion. A full stomach also increases aspiration risk. Slow ventilations with appropriate volume minimize this.

statement: The ventilation rate during CPR with an advanced airway is 10 breaths per minute.
rationale: With an advanced airway in place ventilate at 10 breaths per minute one every 6 seconds asynchronously with compressions. Without advanced airway use 30:2 ratio.

statement: Room air contains approximately 21% oxygen.
rationale: Atmospheric air is approximately 21% oxygen 78% nitrogen and 1% other gases. Supplemental oxygen increases the inspired concentration above this baseline.

statement: A nasal cannula is appropriate for patients who need only low concentrations of supplemental oxygen.
rationale: Nasal cannula delivers 24-44% oxygen at 1-6 LPM. It is comfortable and appropriate for patients with adequate respiratory effort who need mild supplementation.

statement: The reservoir bag on a non-rebreather mask must remain inflated during inspiration.
rationale: The reservoir bag provides the high concentration of oxygen. If it collapses during inspiration the patient is not getting adequate oxygen. Ensure adequate flow to keep it inflated.

statement: Humidified oxygen is preferred for prolonged oxygen administration.
rationale: Supplemental oxygen is dry and can irritate airways. Humidification adds moisture reducing irritation and drying of mucous membranes during extended use.

statement: Pulse oximetry measures oxygen saturation in arterial blood.
rationale: Pulse oximetry uses light absorption to measure the percentage of hemoglobin saturated with oxygen in arterial blood. Normal SpO2 is 95-100% in healthy individuals.

statement: Pulse oximetry can detect carbon monoxide poisoning.
rationale: Pulse oximeters cannot distinguish between oxygen and carbon monoxide bound to hemoglobin. SpO2 may read normal despite life-threatening CO poisoning.

statement: SpO2 below 94% generally indicates the need for supplemental oxygen.
rationale: Normal SpO2 is 95-100%. Saturation below 94% indicates hypoxemia and generally warrants supplemental oxygen. Lower targets may be acceptable for COPD patients.

statement: Asthma involves inflammation and bronchospasm of the lower airways.
rationale: Asthma affects the bronchi and bronchioles causing inflammation bronchospasm and mucus production. This results in wheezing dyspnea and reduced air flow.

statement: COPD patients may rely on low oxygen levels rather than high CO2 to stimulate breathing.
rationale: Some COPD patients with chronic CO2 retention develop hypoxic drive. Their stimulus to breathe becomes low oxygen rather than high CO2. High-flow O2 may depress their respiratory drive.

statement: Pneumonia is an infection of the lungs causing fluid accumulation in alveoli.
rationale: Pneumonia is lung infection from bacteria viruses or other pathogens. It causes inflammation and fluid accumulation in alveoli impairing gas exchange. Signs include fever cough and crackles.

statement: Pulmonary edema is fluid in the lungs often from heart failure.
rationale: Pulmonary edema occurs when fluid accumulates in the alveoli. Left heart failure causes blood to back up into the lungs forcing fluid out. Signs include severe dyspnea and crackles.

statement: Pulmonary embolism is a blood clot blocking pulmonary arteries.
rationale: PE occurs when a clot usually from deep leg veins travels to and blocks pulmonary arteries. It can cause sudden dyspnea chest pain hypoxia and cardiac arrest.

statement: Tension pneumothorax can cause cardiovascular collapse by compressing the heart.
rationale: Tension pneumothorax allows air to accumulate under pressure shifting the mediastinum and compressing the heart and great vessels. This reduces venous return causing cardiovascular collapse.

statement: Flail chest causes paradoxical chest wall movement impairing ventilation.
rationale: Flail chest occurs when multiple rib fractures create a free segment that moves opposite to the rest of the chest. This paradoxical movement impairs ventilation.

statement: Children have higher oxygen consumption relative to body size than adults.
rationale: Children have higher metabolic rates consuming more oxygen per kilogram than adults. This makes them more susceptible to hypoxia and faster to decompensate from respiratory compromise.
